headline: US and Iran reignite their war over the Strait of Hormuz: Trump drops a 20% shipping fee but presses a naval blockade as strikes fly, pushing oil to a one-month high.
the_big_picture: The single thread running through today is the re-escalation of the US-Iran conflict at the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Trump backed off a threatened 20% transit fee but is pushing ahead with a naval blockade while both sides trade strikes and tankers come under attack, sending crude to a one-month high. That oil spike is the hinge for everything downstream: US inflation cooled to 3.5% but officials flagged fresh upside risk, India's inflation just breached its central bank's target, the rupee slid past 96 and the Sensex fell. Running underneath, an AI storyline cuts both ways: IBM shed roughly $70bn in a day after stumbling on AI deals, even as China's AI-driven exports jumped 27% and Nvidia began shipping powerful H200 chips into China.

stories.title: Congo's Ebola outbreak is now the fastest-spreading in history — nearly 1,000 dead in barely two months
key_points: 2,473 confirmed cases and 999 deaths as of July 21, since the outbreak was declared on May 15
Killing people faster than any Ebola outbreak on record, including the 2013-2016 outbreak that killed 11,000+
The strain involved, Bundibugyo virus, has no approved vaccine or treatment — unlike the more common Ebola Zaire strain
More than 60% of deaths are happening in the community, before patients even reach a hospital
stories.background: Ebola is a severe viral disease that causes fever and internal bleeding and kills a large share of the people it infects. The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), in central Africa, has had more Ebola outbreaks than any other country, including the second-largest ever in 2018-2020. Vaccines exist for the most common strain (Zaire), which is why recent outbreaks were brought under control faster than this one.
stories.what_happened: Health authorities have recorded 2,473 confirmed Ebola cases and 999 deaths since the outbreak began on May 15, 2026 — a pace that makes it the fastest-killing Ebola outbreak ever documented, faster even than the historic 2013-2016 West Africa epidemic. The virus behind it, Bundibugyo ebolavirus, is a rarer strain for which there is no approved vaccine or treatment, unlike the Zaire strain vaccines used in recent years. Africa CDC says conflict in the region, resistance from local communities toward health workers, and an inconsistent response have let the virus spread largely unchecked — fewer than 9% of people who had contact with confirmed cases are being tracked and monitored, far below what's needed to contain an outbreak.
stories.why_it_matters: Without a vaccine, containment depends entirely on old-fashioned public health tools — tracing contacts, isolating the sick, safe burials — and those tools are failing here because of conflict and mistrust. A fast-moving, untreatable outbreak in a conflict zone is exactly the scenario global health officials fear most, because it can outrun the response before the world reacts.
stories.ripple_effects: Neighboring countries tighten border screening and travel advisories, aid agencies redirect funding and staff from other crises, and there's a real risk of the outbreak crossing into neighboring states given how few contacts are being monitored. It also puts pressure on vaccine makers and the WHO to fast-track a Bundibugyo-specific vaccine, something that historically takes years.
stories.why_now: The 1,000-death mark and the 'fastest ever' comparison are what's pushing this into headlines today, even though the outbreak has been building since May — it's the kind of grim milestone that forces renewed global attention on a crisis that had been under-covered.
stories.watch_next: Watch whether the WHO declares this a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) — its highest alert level — which would trigger a much bigger international funding and response effort.

key_terms.definition: A rarer strain of Ebola, first identified in Uganda in 2007, for which no vaccine has yet been approved.
Finding and monitoring everyone who was near an infected person, to catch new cases before they spread further.

stories.the_lesson: Vaccines don't just save the people who take them — they also make outbreaks containable. When a disease strain has no vaccine, the same virus that used to be a manageable regional outbreak can become an uncontained crisis, which is why global health funding for 'rare' strains matters even when they aren't currently spreading.

stories.title: Wall Street snaps a 3-day losing streak on a chip-stock rally — but JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon says he wouldn't buy stocks or bonds at these prices
key_points: S&P 500 closed up 0.89% near 7,509, breaking three straight days of losses
Semiconductor stocks jumped on stronger-than-expected export data from South Korea and Taiwan
Brent crude oil hit $91.24 (+2.26%) on Middle East supply fears, a headwind for inflation
JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon says markets are underestimating how big the risks really are
stories.background: The S&P 500 is the benchmark index of America's 500 biggest public companies — when people say 'the market,' this is usually what they mean. It had fallen for three straight sessions as investors grew nervous about the US-Iran conflict and its effect on oil supplies. Jamie Dimon runs JPMorgan Chase, America's biggest bank, and is one of Wall Street's most-watched voices because he sits at the center of the financial system and talks to CEOs and governments constantly.
stories.what_happened: The S&P 500 rose 0.89% to close near 7,509 on Tuesday, its first gain in four days, as semiconductor (computer-chip) stocks rallied on unexpectedly strong export numbers from South Korea and Taiwan — two countries whose chip exports are seen as an early signal of global tech demand. At the same time, Brent crude oil (the global benchmark price for oil) jumped 2.26% to $91.24 a barrel on fears that the US-Iran war could disrupt Middle East oil supply. In a podcast interview recorded July 16 and released this week, Dimon said he wouldn't buy either stocks or long-dated US government bonds (Treasurys) at current prices, warning that investors are underpricing risks from the Ukraine and Middle East wars, US-China tensions, and rising military spending against a backdrop of large government deficits.
stories.why_it_matters: A rally built on just one sector (chips) while oil prices climb is a fragile kind of good news — it doesn't mean the underlying risks (an active war, high government debt) have gone away. When the head of the largest US bank publicly says he wouldn't buy the market, it's a rare, notable break from Wall Street's usual optimism and tends to make other investors more cautious.
stories.ripple_effects: If oil keeps climbing on the Iran war, that pushes up fuel and shipping costs everywhere, feeding inflation and complicating central banks' plans to cut interest rates. A chip-driven rally particularly helps countries like South Korea, Taiwan, and India's own IT/electronics-linked stocks, but leaves the broader market exposed if that one sector stumbles. Dimon's Treasury comments matter because US bond yields (the return investors demand to lend the government money) ripple into mortgage rates, corporate borrowing costs, and currencies worldwide, including the rupee.
stories.why_now: The rally comes right as the US-Iran war enters its 11th day of American strikes, so markets are simultaneously pricing in war-driven oil risk and chip-sector optimism — two forces pulling in opposite directions, which is why Dimon's warning is landing now.
stories.watch_next: Watch Brent crude's path toward $95+ and the Federal Reserve's next meeting (July 28-29) for whether policymakers treat oil-driven inflation as reason to hold off on rate cuts.

key_terms.definition: An index tracking 500 of the largest US public companies — the most common shorthand for 'the US stock market.'
Bonds (IOUs) issued by the US government; their yield is a benchmark interest rate for the whole economy.
The global reference price for oil, used to price roughly two-thirds of the world's traded crude.

stories.the_lesson: A single-sector rally (like chips) can mask broader fragility — always ask what's NOT participating in a market's 'good day,' since a rally with narrow leadership tends to reverse faster than one with broad support.
stories.market_link: Directly moves US indices, oil-linked stocks (energy majors), and via Brent crude, India's import bill, the rupee, and inflation-sensitive sectors.

stories.title: Tropical Storm Bertha makes landfall near Louisiana — but the damage looks limited so far
key_points: Bertha came ashore/grazed the Louisiana coast Wednesday with winds around 50 mph, down from 60 mph the night before
It's expected to track over New Orleans Wednesday night, then head toward Houston before dissipating
Forecasters warn of 1-4 inches of rain (isolated 6in) and storm surge up to 4 feet along the coast
Early reports suggest impacts are milder than initially feared, though flash flooding risk continues
stories.background: Bertha is the second named storm of the 2026 Atlantic hurricane season, which runs June through November. Forecasters had flagged El Niño — a Pacific Ocean warming pattern that tends to increase high-altitude wind shear over the Atlantic — as a factor that could suppress storm formation this year, making Bertha's slow crawl along the Gulf Coast a real-time test of that forecast.
stories.what_happened: Tropical Storm Bertha weakened slightly to about 50 mph sustained winds as it crawled west along the northern Gulf Coast, making landfall or grazing southeast Louisiana on Wednesday afternoon. The storm is forecast to pass near New Orleans Wednesday night before continuing toward Houston and dissipating by the end of the week, dropping 1 to 4 inches of rain (isolated pockets up to 6 inches) and pushing storm surge as high as 4 feet in spots.
stories.why_it_matters: Even a weak tropical storm can cause outsized damage in low-lying Gulf Coast cities like New Orleans and Houston, where storm surge and urban flash flooding are the main risks — not wind speed. The Gulf Coast is also home to a large share of US oil refining capacity, so any storm tracking through it gets watched by energy markets regardless of its category.
stories.ripple_effects: Refineries and ports along the Louisiana and Texas coast may pause or slow operations as a precaution, a routine but watched event for fuel markets. Coastal residents face localized flooding and possible power outages; airlines and school districts in the storm's path often issue precautionary delays or closures.
stories.why_now: The storm reached the coast today after two days tracking through the Gulf of Mexico, with forecasters narrowing its landfall window to Wednesday afternoon/evening.
stories.watch_next: Whether flash flooding materializes in New Orleans and Houston as Bertha's rain bands move through over the next 48 hours.

key_terms.definition: A rise in seawater level pushed ashore by a storm's winds — often more dangerous than the wind itself, since it can flood low-lying coastal areas even from a 'weak' storm.
A periodic warming of the central Pacific Ocean that changes global weather patterns — it tends to increase wind shear over the Atlantic, which can suppress hurricane formation.

stories.the_lesson: A storm's category (or wind speed) doesn't tell you how dangerous it is — for coastal, low-lying cities, slow-moving rain and storm surge from even a 'weak' tropical storm can matter more than the wind number in the headline.
stories.market_link: Gulf Coast oil refiners and ports (a large share of US refining capacity) are watched for precautionary shutdowns, which can briefly move fuel and crude prices.

stories.title: US carries out an 11th straight night of strikes on Iran as Trump threatens to bomb a power plant for every ship Iran hits
key_points: US warplanes and ships struck Iranian military sites for about 75 minutes — the 11th consecutive night of strikes
Trump says he'll hit an Iranian power plant every time Iran attacks a commercial ship
Iran's health ministry says 50 civilians have been killed and 500 wounded by the US strikes so far
A 4th American service member's death in the war has now been confirmed, with over 500 US troops wounded
stories.background: The US and Israel opened this war on Iran back in late February 2026, and Washington has since run a nightly bombing campaign aimed at Iran's military, naval and nuclear-linked sites. A ceasefire announced in mid-June collapsed within weeks after Iran-linked strikes on tankers near the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway that a fifth of the world's oil passes through. Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth told Congress this week the war has already cost $37.5 billion, and yesterday Trump threatened to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site after Tehran struck three more Gulf allies (Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan).
stories.what_happened: CENTCOM (the US military's Middle East command) carried out its 11th straight night of strikes on Iran late Tuesday, hitting military operations centers, aircraft hangars, drone-storage sites and logistics infrastructure meant to degrade Iran's ability to threaten shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's health ministry says the campaign has now killed 50 civilians and wounded 500. The Pentagon separately confirmed a 4th American service member has died in the conflict, with US wounded now well over 500. Trump added a new threat overnight: for every commercial ship Iran hits, the US will bomb an Iranian power plant.
stories.why_it_matters: This is no longer a short punitive campaign — it's a grinding, open-ended war with a rising American body count and a mounting bill ($37.5bn and climbing, with the Pentagon separately asking Congress for $67bn more). Trump's new 'a power plant for every ship' threat also marks a shift toward directly targeting Iranian civilian infrastructure, which would be a major escalation with humanitarian and legal consequences.
stories.ripple_effects: Shipping insurers are already pricing in war risk for tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf oil exporters (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq) face rising costs rerouting or insuring cargo. Oil prices stay elevated on the supply-disruption risk, which feeds into inflation worldwide. Gulf states hosting US bases (Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan) are being pulled deeper into the conflict as Iranian retaliation targets, straining Washington's regional alliances.
stories.why_now: The strikes have continued nightly since the ceasefire collapsed roughly a month ago; last night's round and Trump's new threat come as the Pentagon faces congressional questioning over the war's mounting cost and no exit strategy in sight.
stories.watch_next: Whether Trump follows through on the power-plant threat after the next Iranian strike on shipping — that would mark a major escalation toward hitting civilian energy infrastructure.

key_terms.definition: US Central Command — the US military's command overseeing operations across the Middle East, including strikes on Iran.
A narrow shipping chokepoint between Iran and Oman that roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes through — any disruption there ripples through global energy prices.

stories.the_lesson: Wars fought to 'degrade capability' rarely have a clean off-ramp — without a negotiated settlement, nightly strikes can grind on for months, and each side's escalatory threats (like targeting power plants) tend to widen rather than shorten the conflict.
stories.market_link: Crude oil (Brent/WTI) and tanker-insurance costs stay elevated on Strait of Hormuz risk; defense stocks and Gulf-exposed shipping and refining names remain sensitive to escalation headlines.

thread.previously: Yesterday, Trump threatened to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site after Iran struck three more Gulf allies (Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan), as the Pentagon told Congress the war had already cost $37.5 billion.
thread.changed: The US has now completed an 11th consecutive night of strikes, Iran's health ministry has given its first civilian casualty count (50 killed, 500 wounded), a 4th American service member's death was confirmed, and Trump has issued a new, more specific threat: a power plant hit for every ship Iran attacks.
